title: Simple & Compound Interest
subtitle: Welcome to Today's Lesson
content:
What is Interest?

  • Interest = the cost of borrowing money, or the reward for saving it
  • Banks pay you interest for using your money
  • You pay banks interest for using theirs

Two Types:

Simple Interest vs Compound Interest

They sound similar, but the difference can mean thousands of dollars over time.

title: Understanding Interest
subtitle: A Real-World Picture
content:
Interest in Everyday Life

You lend your friend $100

At the end of the year, they give you back $105

That extra $5 = Interest (5%)

The Big Question:

How does that percentage get calculated each year?

That's where Simple and Compound interest split apart.

title: Simple Interest
subtitle: The Formula
content:
I = P × r × t
IInterest earned
PPrincipal (starting amount)
rAnnual interest rate (as decimal)
tTime (in years)

Key Conversions:

  • 5% → 0.05 | 12% → 0.12
  • 6 months → 0.5 years | 18 months → 1.5 years

title: Simple Interest — Example 1
subtitle: $1,000 at 5% for 3 Years
content:
I = 1,000 × 0.05 × 3 = $150

Total balance: $1,150

YearInterestBalance
1$50$1,050
2$50$1,100
3$50$1,150

Same $50 every year — only calculates on original principal

title: Simple Interest — Example 2
subtitle: $2,500 at 4% for 5 Years
content:
I = 2,500 × 0.04 × 5 = 2,500 × 0.2 = $500

Total balance: $3,000

YearInterest
1$100
2$100
3$100
4$100
5$100

Flat, predictable, constant. That's simple interest.

title: Think About It
subtitle: Quick Mental Math Challenge
content:
Try This:

3% simple interest on $10,000 for 10 years

I = 10,000 × 0.03 × 10 = ?

I = 10,000 × 0.3 = $3,000

Balance: $10,000 → $13,000

Hold this number — we'll compare it to compound interest soon.

title: Simple Interest in Real Life
subtitle: Car Loans
content:
Car Loan Example

Borrow $20,000 for a car

Rate: 6% simple interest

Time: 5 years


I = 20,000 × 0.06 × 5 = $6,000

Total repayment: $26,000

Interest calculated on the original $20,000 only — doesn't matter when you pay.

title: Compound Interest
subtitle: Money Making Money on Money
content:
With compound interest, you earn interest on your original deposit AND the interest you've already earned.

It's like a snowball rolling downhill. 🏔️

A = P × (1 + r/n)n×t
ATotal amount
PPrincipal
rAnnual rate (decimal)
nTimes compounded per year
tTime in years

title: Compounding Frequency
subtitle: What Does n Equal?
content:
Compoundingn value
Annually1
Semi-annually2
Quarterly4
Monthly12
Daily365

The more frequently it compounds, the more interest you earn.

title: Compound Interest — Worked Example
subtitle: $1,000 at 5% Compounded Annually for 3 Years
content:
YearCalculationBalance
1$1,000 × 1.05$1,050.00
2$1,050 × 1.05$1,102.50
3$1,102.50 × 1.05$1,157.63

Year-by-Year Comparison:

YearSimpleCompound
1$50.00$50.00
2$50.00$52.50
3$50.00$55.13

The gap widens every year!

title: Monthly vs Annual Compounding
subtitle: $1,000 at 5% for 3 Years
content:
Monthly Compounding

A = 1,000 × (1 + 0.05/12)36

= 1,000 × (1.004167)36

= 1,000 × 1.16162

= $1,161.62

Simple
$1,150.00
Annual
$1,157.63
Monthly
$1,161.62
Daily
$1,161.82

title: The Power of Compound Interest
subtitle: $5,000 at 8% for 20 Years
content:
Simple Interest

I = 5,000 × 0.08 × 20 = $8,000

$13,000

Compound Interest

A = 5,000 × 1.08²⁰

$23,305
$10,305 more — same money, same rate, same time.
Simple grew by $8,000. Compound grew by $18,305.
Compound interest more than doubles the gain.

title: Starting Early — The Most Powerful Move
subtitle: Person A (age 20) vs Person B (age 30)
content:
Person A — Starts at 20

$200/month for 40 years

Total invested: $96,000

$525,000

Person B — Starts at 30

$200/month for 30 years

Total invested: $72,000

$243,000
Person A invested only $24,000 more but ended up with $282,000 more.
Those first 10 years created a lead that Person B could never catch up to.

title: Compound Interest — The Dark Side
subtitle: Credit Cards
content:
Credit Card Interest Works Against You

Buy a phone: $1,200 on credit card

Rate: 24% compounded daily, no payments for 1 year


A = 1,200 × (1 + 0.24/365)365 = $1,525.44

Interest: $325 — almost another phone!


After 3 years with no payments: ≈ $2,454

Your $1,200 phone now costs over $2,400.

Compound interest is your best friend when saving, your worst enemy when borrowing.

title: Building Wealth — $100/Month
subtitle: Consistent Saving + Compound Interest
content:
$100/month at 5% compounded monthly

AgeTotal InvestedBalanceInterest Earned
25 (7 yrs)$8,400$9,870$1,470
30 (12 yrs)$14,400$18,400$4,000
40 (22 yrs)$26,400$41,100$14,700
65 (47 yrs)$56,400$232,000$175,600
Put in $56,400. Got back $232,000. That's compound interest.

title: The Rule of 72
subtitle: Quick Doubling Time Estimate
content:
Doubling Time ≈ 72 ÷ Interest Rate
Rate72 ÷ RateYears to Double
4%72 ÷ 418 years
6%72 ÷ 612 years
8%72 ÷ 89 years
10%72 ÷ 107.2 years
12%72 ÷ 126 years
$5,000 at 8% → Rule of 72 says ~9 years to double
Actual: 5,000 × 1.08⁹ = $9,995 ✓

title: Interest Rate vs APY
subtitle: Understanding What Banks Advertise
content:
APY = Annual Percentage Yield

  • APY accounts for compounding — it's the effective rate
  • The nominal rate might be slightly lower
  • Compounding brings it up to the advertised APY

When comparing savings accounts:

Always compare APY to APY

That's the true apples-to-apples comparison.

title: Test Practice — Simple Interest
subtitle: Maria's Deposit
content:
Problem:

Maria deposits $8,000 at 3.5% simple interest for 4 years.

Step 1: P = 8,000, r = 0.035, t = 4

Step 2: I = 8,000 × 0.035 × 4 = $1,120

Step 3: Total = 8,000 + 1,120 = $9,120

Same Problem — Compound (Monthly):

A = 8,000 × (1 + 0.035/12)48

= 8,000 × 1.1503 = $9,202.40

Compound earned $82.40 more

title: Solving for Different Variables
subtitle: I = P × r × t — Rearranged
content:
Solve for Time:

$6,000 needs to earn $1,800 at 5%

t = I ÷ (P × r) = 1,800 ÷ (6,000 × 0.05) = 1,800 ÷ 300 = 6 years

Solve for Rate:

$4,000 earns $960 in 4 years

r = I ÷ (P × t) = 960 ÷ (4,000 × 4) = 960 ÷ 16,000 = 6%

Solve for Principal:

Earn $2,100 at 7% in 3 years

P = I ÷ (r × t) = 2,100 ÷ (0.07 × 3) = 2,100 ÷ 0.21 = $10,000

title: The Graduation Gift
subtitle: $3,000 at 6% Compounded Quarterly for 10 Years
content:
P = 3,000 | r = 0.06 | n = 4 | t = 10


A = 3,000 × (1 + 0.06/4)40

= 3,000 × (1.015)40

= 3,000 × 1.8140

= $5,442

Simple Interest
$4,800
Compound Interest
$5,442

$642 more — your graduation gift nearly doubled without you doing anything.

title: The Twin Experiment
subtitle: Alex vs Jordan — Starting Early Wins
content:
Alex — Starts at 18

$150/month for 40 years at 7%

Total invested: $72,000

$395,000

Jordan — Starts at 28

$300/month for 30 years at 7%

Total invested: $108,000

$365,000
Jordan invested $36,000 MORE but ended up with $30,000 LESS.
The 10-year head start beats double the monthly investment.

title: Key Rules to Remember
subtitle: Your Cheat Sheet
content:
1. Convert % to decimal — 5% → 0.05, 12% → 0.12
  2. Time in years — 18 months → 1.5, 6 months → 0.5
  3. Know your n — Annual=1, Semi=2, Quarterly=4, Monthly=12, Daily=365
  4. Label everything — P=?, r=?, t=?, n=?
  5. Sanity check — Does your answer make sense?
  6. Know the direction — Saving? Want compound. Borrowing? Prefer simple.
Simple: I = P × r × t
Compound: A = P × (1 + r/n)n×t

title: Lesson Review
subtitle: What We Covered Today
content:
• ✅ Interest — cost of borrowing / reward for saving
  • ✅ Simple Interest — linear, same amount each year, on principal only
  • ✅ Compound Interest — exponential, interest on interest
  • ✅ Real-world applications — savings, loans, credit cards, investments
  • ✅ Rule of 72 — quick doubling estimate
  • ✅ Starting early — the most powerful financial decision

Time + Compound Interest = Wealth

Start early. Let it compound. Be patient.

title: Practice Problems
subtitle: Show Your Work!
content:
📝 Calculate BOTH simple and compound interest:

  1. $5,000 at 4% for 6 years (compound monthly)
  2. $12,000 at 3.5% for 8 years (compound annually)
  3. $800 at 7% for 3 years (compound quarterly)
  4. How long to double $10,000 at 6%? (Use Rule of 72)
  5. What rate turns $4,000 into $5,200 simple interest in 5 years?

Label variables • Show formulas • Compare results • Check your decimals
